text: This is better than the previous iteration, but I wish the geometric pattern wasn't so regular.

snapshot.title: DOM Bake Test
snapshot.subtitle: Phase 12 proof
snapshot.prompt: Abstract grid composition of nine square panels, each showing a slightly different geometric variation — a population of candidates under selection pressure. Flat vector style with subtle gradients. Muted palette of deep indigo, teal, and warm off-white. Minimal lines, no text, no literal machinery or AI imagery. Slight grain texture. Evokes iteration, refinement, and design-system evolution without being on-the-nose.

text: Pushed the prompt away from the explicit nine-panel grid: now it asks for an irregular cluster of panels at varied scales and orientations, with overlapping shapes and some panels fading at edges. Same palette and grain. If it is still too ordered, tell me in which direction — more scattered, more organic curves, or fewer panels larger.
I really like this version. How can we select this as a preferred variation as part of our evolution by artificial selection paradigm?

snapshot.title: Title TK
snapshot.subtitle: Phase 12 proof
snapshot.prompt: Irregular cluster of geometric panels at varied scales and orientations — a loose population of candidates under selection pressure, not arranged on a grid. Flat vector style with subtle gradients. Muted palette of deep indigo, teal, and warm off-white. Organic spacing, overlapping shapes at slight angles, some panels fading at their edges. Minimal lines, no text, no literal machinery or AI imagery. Slight grain texture. Evokes iteration, refinement, and organic variation without being on-the-nose.

text: You already did step one — the 5-star rating is the selection signal. Next: click "Save as template" on this entry. That captures the prompt + preset + provider as a reusable library record. From then on, the Generate drawer's Template dropdown will show it (with its fitness score updating as future generations using it get rated). When you want a variant, Fork the template — it clones with a parent reference, so the library grows as a lineage tree where good prompts beget good prompts and low-rated ones fade out of the default picker. The lineage across actual generations is also tracked (this entry's parent chain goes back to the original grid attempt via parentEntryId), so you can always see the path that led here.
